meta.description: Smuggler career questline — from desperate runner to district legend, 100 nodes spanning contraband runs, dangerous clients, crew management, and the moral cost of living outside the law

jsmg_001.title: The First Run
text: The package is the size of a shoebox, wrapped in static-dampening foil, and heavier than it looks. Your contact — a woman who calls herself Nix and whose face you've already forgotten because that's how she wants it — hands it to you in the maintenance corridor beneath Platform 7.

"Block 19 to the market district. Avoid the transit patrols on the north corridor. Avoid the Bone Saints checkpoint on Seventh. Avoid eye contact with anyone wearing corporate security grey." She pauses. "Actually, just avoid everyone."
"What's in the package?"

"Rule one: don't ask what's in the package. Rule two: don't open the package. Rule three: if you get caught, you've never met me, you've never heard of me, and this package fell from the sky into your hands by divine intervention."
She gives you an address. A time. A payment upon delivery: forty credits. Not much. But more than you've seen in a while, and the work doesn't require you to bleed for it. Probably.

choices.text: Take the run. Forty credits is forty credits
Ask about the patrol schedules first

jsmg_002.title: The North Corridor
text: The north corridor runs beneath the transit lines — a maintenance tunnel that was decommissioned three years ago when the transit authority decided that maintaining infrastructure in the Lower District wasn't cost-effective. Now it serves a different purpose: the main smuggling artery between the residential blocks and the market district.

The tunnel is dark, damp, and smells of machine oil and rust. Your footsteps echo against metal grating. The package sits in your pack, a weight that feels heavier with every step because you are carrying something illegal through a space designed for rats and criminals, and tonight you are the criminal.

Halfway through, you hear voices ahead. Two patrol officers, their helmet lights cutting white beams through the darkness. They're doing a sweep — random or scheduled, it doesn't matter. They're between you and the exit.
Your options crystallize with the clarity of adrenaline.

choices.text: Find a side passage and wait them out
Bluff — walk past like you belong here

jsmg_003.title: Know Your Route
text: Nix looks at you with an expression that falls somewhere between approval and impatience. "Patrol shifts change at midnight. You've got a forty-minute window on the north corridor between 00:20 and 01:00. After that, the night shift sweeps through and they're thorough."

She pulls up a rough map on a cracked datapad. "The Bone Saints checkpoint is on Seventh between Block 16 and 18. They tax runners — usually ten percent of cargo value, but since they don't know the value and you're not going to tell them, it becomes whatever they feel like charging. Best to avoid them entirely."

"Corporate security rotates on a three-hour cycle in the market district. Shift change at 02:00 — that's your delivery window. Get in, drop the package, get out before the fresh shift's scanners come online."
She folds the datapad. "Questions? No? Good. Don't die. You're the third runner I've hired this week and the first two are in a holding cell."

choices.text: Head out on the route with the patrol schedule memorized
Scout the route yourself first — never trust someone else's intel

jsmg_004.title: The Side Passage
text: You slip into a maintenance alcove — a recess in the tunnel wall that once housed junction controls and now houses you, pressed flat against cold metal, breathing as quietly as physics allows. The patrol passes within arm's reach. You can hear their conversation: complaints about shift schedules, a debate about a sports match, the mundane chatter of people doing a job they don't particularly care about.
They pass. Their lights fade. You wait another thirty seconds because patience is the first skill of smuggling, and then you move.

The rest of the route is clear. You emerge from the tunnel into the market district through a drainage grate that Nix marked on her map, and you find the delivery address: a noodle shop on the edge of the market, closed at this hour, with a back door that opens when you knock three times.
A hand takes the package. A credstick appears. Forty credits, as promised. The hand withdraws. The door closes. Transaction complete.

choices.text: Return to Nix for another job
Keep some of the cargo for yourself — Nix won't notice a small skim

jsmg_005.title: The Bluff
text: You walk toward the patrol with the specific confidence of someone who has every right to be here. Body language is the first lie — before you open your mouth, your posture, your pace, your expression have already told a story. Tonight's story is: maintenance worker, end of shift, heading home.
"Hey," one officer calls out. "Tunnel's closed. What are you doing down here?"

"Valve check on the secondary water main," you say, naming something that sounds plausible because you once watched an actual maintenance worker do exactly that. "Dispatch sent me down at shift end. Overtime pay." You roll your eyes in the universal expression of someone mildly annoyed at a bureaucracy.
The officer shines his light on you, then on your pack. Your heart rate spikes but your face doesn't change. "Valve check takes a backpack?"
"Tools. Gaskets. Lunch I never ate." You shrug. "Want to see my gaskets?"
Nobody wants to see your gaskets. The officer waves you through. You walk past at a steady pace and do not look back and do not breathe until you're around the corner.

choices.text: Complete the delivery and collect your pay
Report the patrol weakness to Nix — good intel is its own currency

jsmg_006.title: Building a Reputation
text: You find Nix at a bar in Sector 7 — a place where the lighting is dim enough to conduct business and the bartender's memory is conveniently poor. She's drinking something clear and odorless, which is either water or something that would dissolve your stomach lining. With Nix, it could be either.
"You made it," she says. Not impressed, exactly. More like: you met the minimum threshold for continued existence. "Clean run?"
"Clean enough."

"Good. Because clean runs get you more runs. Dirty runs get you dead or jailed, and I don't visit either." She slides a datapad across the table. Three more jobs. Increasing pay, increasing risk, the standard progression of an industry that rewards survival with more opportunities to not survive.

Over the next weeks, you run packages. Small at first — data chips, pharmaceutical containers, sealed electronics. Then larger — crates that require two people to carry, shipments that need vehicles, deliveries that span multiple districts. Each run teaches you something: a new route, a new contact, a new way to read a patrol's body language.

choices.text: Keep running for Nix — she's reliable
Start taking jobs from other contacts too

jsmg_007.title: Nix's Network
text: Loyalty to Nix earns you access to her network — a web of contacts, safe houses, and supply chains that spans the Lower District and reaches tendrils into the mid-levels. She introduces you to her operation piece by piece, each introduction a test of trust.

You meet Ferris, who runs the warehouse where incoming cargo is sorted and repackaged. You meet Ghost, a former transit worker who knows every tunnel, duct, and maintenance corridor in the district. You meet the Broker, a faceless voice on an encrypted channel who matches cargo with clients.

"I've been doing this for twelve years," Nix tells you one evening. "In that time, I've had forty-seven runners. You know how many are still alive and free?" She holds up a hand. Five fingers. "Six, counting you. This job has a high turnover rate for reasons that should be obvious."
"Why do you keep doing it?"
"Because the alternative is working in a foundry until my lungs give out. At least smuggling is interesting."

choices.text: Ask about the bigger jobs — you're ready
Ask about working other districts. More territory means more opportunity

jsmg_008.title: Freelance
text: You diversify. The black market is full of people who need things moved from one place to another without official scrutiny, and your reputation — small but growing — opens doors. You take jobs from independent dealers, gang operations, and a mysterious client who communicates only through dead drops and pays in untraceable credsticks.

The freedom is intoxicating. No boss, no network, no obligation beyond the job at hand. You choose your clients, your routes, your risks. The money is better but the safety net is gone — no Nix to warn you about patrol changes, no Ghost to plot alternate routes, no network to bail you out when things go wrong.
Nix notices your freelancing and says nothing, which is worse than if she'd said something. Her silence is the silence of a professional reassessing an asset.
"You're building a reputation," she finally says. "Make sure it's the right one. In this business, your name is either an asset or a liability. There's no middle ground."

choices.text: The freelance life suits you. Keep building connections
Look for a partner. Solo runs are profitable but risky

jsmg_009.title: The Ambush
text: It happens on what should be a routine run. Medical supplies — legitimate ones, diverted from a corporate shipment to a clinic in Block 22. You're in the north corridor when the lights go out and someone puts a gun to the back of your head.
"Package. Floor. Now."
There are three of them. Not patrol — their gear is mismatched, civilian. Hijackers. People who prey on runners because runners carry valuable cargo and can't report the theft to anyone.

You lower the package. Your mind races through options: fight, flee, negotiate. Fighting three armed people in a dark tunnel is a losing proposition. Fleeing means losing the cargo. Negotiating requires something to negotiate with.

choices.text: Hand over the package — your life is worth more than any cargo
"You know whose cargo this is? Take it and find out."
Drop to the floor and fight in the darkness

jsmg_010.title: The Cost of Survival
text: You let them take it. The package disappears into the darkness with the hijackers, and you stand in the tunnel with nothing but your life and the knowledge that the medical supplies meant for Block 22 are now gone.

Nix takes the news with the cold pragmatism of someone who has calculated losses before. "The cargo is replaceable. You aren't. Yet." She covers the cost from her reserves and reshuffles the delivery schedule. The clinic in Block 22 waits another week for supplies that people are dying without.

The hijackers bother you. Not the loss — losses happen — but the specificity. They knew your route, your timing, your cargo. Someone fed them information. Someone in Nix's network, or someone watching it from outside.
"We have a leak," you tell Nix.
"I know," she says. "I've known for two weeks. I was using it to feed false information. But now they've escalated from surveillance to robbery, and that changes the equation."

choices.text: Help Nix find the leak
Use the leak as an opportunity — feed false information through it

jsmg_011.title: The Bluff in the Dark
text: You name-drop. Not a specific name — that would be verifiable — but the implication of a name. A powerful name. The kind of name that makes hijackers reconsider their career choices.

"This cargo belongs to people who track what happens to their shipments," you say, keeping your voice level. "People who take inventory losses personally. You can take the package. But in three days, they'll know who took it, and in four days, you'll wish you'd picked a different tunnel to work."

The silence stretches. The gun at the back of your head doesn't waver, but the person holding it is thinking. In the smuggling world, reputation is currency, and the implication that this cargo is connected to someone dangerous enough to hunt them down is a price they're calculating against the value of the package.

"Let's go," one of them says. The gun withdraws. Footsteps recede into darkness. You stand alone in the tunnel, heart hammering, package intact, having survived on nothing but the particular brand of confidence that smuggling teaches: the confidence that your words can stop a bullet.

choices.text: Complete the delivery and report the ambush to Nix
Track the ambushers yourself. They might lead to bigger opportunities

jsmg_012.title: The Fight
text: You drop flat. The gun fires — the shot sparks off the tunnel ceiling above where your head was a half-second ago. In the darkness, you have one advantage: you know this tunnel. You've run it a dozen times. You know where the junction boxes protrude from the walls, where the grating changes from solid to mesh, where a person can squeeze into an alcove and become invisible.

You fight dirty because clean fighting is a luxury of people who aren't outnumbered in a sewer. You use the darkness, the terrain, your knowledge of the space. One hijacker trips over a raised section of grating and goes down. You take his weapon. The second one runs. The third fires twice more and then decides that the package isn't worth dying for.

You emerge from the tunnel bruised, cut, and alive, with the package intact and a hijacker's weapon in your belt. The weapon is cheap, poorly maintained, and might explode if you fire it. You keep it anyway. In the Lower District, a bad weapon is still a weapon.

The fight changes something in you. Not the violence — you've seen violence before. The knowledge that you chose to fight for cargo. That the package mattered more to you than your safety. That is the moment you became a real smuggler.

choices.text: Complete the delivery and tell Nix about the ambush
Keep quiet about the ambush and handle it yourself next time

jsmg_013.title: The Leak
text: The leak is Ferris, the warehouse manager. Nix discovers this through the oldest method in intelligence: she feeds different information to different people and sees which version reaches the hijackers. Ferris received route details that matched the ambush. The betrayal is confirmed.

Ferris has been selling route information to a rival operation run by a smuggler named Crane — a mid-level operator who has been trying to undercut Nix's network for months. The hijackers were Crane's people, using Ferris's intelligence to intercept shipments and redirect them through Crane's pipeline.
"What do you want to do about Ferris?" Nix asks. Her voice is flat, professional. This is a business decision, not a personal one.
"And Crane?"
"Crane is a problem I'll handle. Ferris is a problem I'm asking you to handle. Consider it a promotion test."

choices.text: Confront Ferris. Give him a chance to explain
Cut Ferris out of the network. No conversation needed

jsmg_014.title: Ferris's Story
text: You find Ferris in the warehouse. When you tell him what you know, his face goes through a rapid sequence: denial, fear, and then something unexpected — relief. The relief of a man who has been carrying a secret that was crushing him.

"Crane's got my daughter," Ferris says. "Not physically — he doesn't need to. He got her a job in the mid-levels. Legal employment, real housing, a future. All I had to do was feed him route data. When I tried to stop, he said the job would disappear and she'd be back down here."

The story is either true or Ferris is a better actor than any smuggler you've met. You look at his hands — they're shaking. You look at his eyes — they're wet. Either this is genuine desperation or a performance worthy of an award.
"I'm sorry," he says. "I'm sorry for the runs that got hit. I'm sorry for the people who didn't get their medicine. I just... she's my kid. She's my kid and she finally has a chance."

choices.text: "We'll find another way to protect your daughter. But the leaks stop now."
"I understand. But I can't let this continue. You're out, Ferris."

jsmg_015.title: Clean Cut
text: You don't give Ferris a conversation. You change the warehouse access codes, redirect the supply chain through a new location, and send Ferris a message through Ghost: you're out. Don't come back. Don't contact anyone in the network. Consider the fact that you're walking away healthy as a professional courtesy.

It's cold. It's efficient. It's exactly what a smuggling operation requires: clean decisions made without sentiment. Nix nods approvingly when you report it. "Good. Fast. No drama. That's how you handle a breach."

But you wonder, later, sitting in your container in the dark: what was Ferris's reason? Was he greedy? Desperate? Coerced? You'll never know, because you didn't ask, and in the smuggling business, not asking is the first rule and the hardest one.

Not asking keeps you safe. Not asking lets you move packages without knowing what's inside. Not asking protects you from truths that would make the work impossible. But not asking also makes you something less than fully human, and the trade-off sits in your stomach like a stone.

choices.text: Focus on the next job. Sentiment is a liability
Mourn properly. Even smugglers need to honor the fallen

jsmg_016.title: The Cargo That Breathes
text: Nix has a special job. High pay, high risk, high discretion. She explains the parameters without explaining the cargo, which is normal, except that this time the parameters include: "Maintain temperature. Ensure ventilation. Handle with extreme care."
Temperature. Ventilation.
"It's a person," you say.

Nix doesn't deny it. "A person who needs to move from the Lower District to the skyport without being scanned, registered, or seen. Someone who doesn't officially exist and needs to keep it that way."

Human smuggling. The line you've been approaching since your first run, the line that separates moving things from moving people. On one side, you're a courier with flexible ethics. On the other side, you're either a savior or a trafficker, and the distinction depends entirely on context.
"Who are they?" you ask, breaking rule one.
"Someone who will die if they stay. That's all you need to know."

choices.text: Take the job. If they'll die otherwise, you're saving a life
Refuse. You move cargo, not people

jsmg_017.title: The Passenger
text: The passenger is a woman in her thirties, thin, with the haunted alertness of someone who has been hunted. She carries nothing — no bag, no datapad, no possessions. Just herself and the clothes on her back and the terror behind her eyes.

Her name is Adaeze. She is a former Meridian data analyst who discovered something she shouldn't have — financial records linking the corporation to the bioweapon program in the Lower District. She tried to report it internally. The response was immediate and violent: her stack was flagged for seizure, her identity was erased from the system, and a corporate security team was dispatched to collect her.

She has been hiding in the tunnels for two weeks. Without a stack, without an identity, without a way to leave the district through any official channel. She needs to reach the skyport, where a contact will arrange passage to a city beyond Meridian's reach.
You look at her and think: this is what cargo looks like when it has eyes. When it's afraid. When it's trusting you with the only thing it has left.

choices.text: Plan the route carefully — this cargo can't be replaced if lost
Scout alternative routes in case the primary is compromised

jsmg_018.title: The Line
text: You refuse. Nix takes it without argument — she's a professional and professionals don't force unwilling participants into high-risk operations. "Fair enough," she says. "I'll find another runner."

But the refusal sits wrong. You lie in your container that night and think about the person who needs to move — the person who will die if they stay — and you think about rules. Rule one: don't ask what's in the package. You broke that rule. And now you know, and knowing changes everything.

Because a package doesn't care if you refuse. A package doesn't die in a tunnel because the runner who could have saved them decided that moving people was across a moral line drawn in the comfort of a bar.
At 2 AM, you go back to Nix. "I'll do it," you say. She looks at you and nods, unsurprised.
"I knew you would. I just needed you to arrive there on your own."

choices.text: Meet the passenger
Ask for more money. Human cargo is higher risk, higher pay

jsmg_019.title: The Human Route
text: Moving a person is nothing like moving a package. Packages don't get tired. Packages don't cough at the wrong moment. Packages don't freeze when they hear patrol footsteps and need you to physically move them because their legs have stopped working from fear.

Adaeze is brave — braver than most people you've met — but bravery has limits, and those limits are tested in a sewer tunnel at 3 AM with corporate security searching for you somewhere above. You guide her through the network of maintenance passages that Ghost mapped, through spaces so narrow you have to turn sideways, through sections where the water reaches your thighs and the darkness is absolute.
"How do you do this?" she whispers.
"Practice," you say. "And a willingness to get wet."

You reach the skyport junction at 04:00. The contact is waiting — a dockworker with a maintenance badge and a cargo manifest that lists Adaeze as 'recycled electronics, non-hazardous.' She boards a freight shuttle without looking back, and you stand in the pre-dawn darkness and wonder if you just saved a life or enabled a fugitive, and whether there's really a difference.

choices.text: Return to Nix. You've proven yourself for bigger work
Take a break between jobs. Exhaustion makes runners sloppy

jsmg_020.title: Rising Through
text: The human run changes your status in Nix's network. You are no longer a runner — you are a specialist. The jobs that come to you now are the ones that require judgment, adaptability, and the ability to improvise when the plan falls apart, which it always does.

You learn the smuggler's paradoxes: the safest route is the one nobody uses, but nobody uses it because it's the most dangerous. The best cargo is the kind nobody wants, until everybody wants it. The most reliable client is the one who pays on time, which means the ones you trust least.

Over the next months, your reputation solidifies. You are the runner who bluffs patrol officers. The one who fought off hijackers in the dark. The one who moved a person through corporate security like a ghost. The word for you in the smuggling underground is 'reliable,' which sounds mundane until you understand that in an industry of liars and thieves, reliability is the rarest currency there is.

choices.text: You're ready for Rank 2. Ask Nix about the courier operations
Negotiate better terms first. You've earned more than the standard rate

jsmg_021.title: Rank 2: The Courier
text: Nix promotes you to courier. The distinction is not just semantic — runners carry what they're given; couriers choose their cargo, negotiate their rates, and manage their own routes. You are now a small business operating inside a larger criminal enterprise, with all the autonomy and risk that implies.

Your first courier assignment is a high-value shipment: prototype neural interface components stolen from a Meridian Research lab. The client is a tech collective in the old quarter that reverse-engineers corporate technology for public use. The cargo is worth more than everything you've earned as a runner combined.
"Don't lose these," Nix says. "The client has been waiting three months. And the people who stole them from Meridian are relying on you to make their risk worthwhile."
Rank 2. Courier. The packages get bigger, the money gets better, and the consequences of failure get worse.

choices.text: Plan the route for the neural interface delivery
Inspect the cargo yourself before accepting. Trust, but verify

jsmg_022.title: High-Value Cargo
text: The neural interface run goes clean. You deliver the components to the tech collective, collect a payment that makes your previous earnings look like pocket change, and walk away with the satisfaction of a job well done and the anxiety of knowing that the next job will be harder.

It is. The next client is a weapons dealer — not the small-time black market variety, but a professional who supplies arms to multiple factions across the district. He wants you to move a shipment of military-grade pulse weapons from the scrap yards to a warehouse in the old quarter.

Weapons. Another line. You've moved medicine, electronics, people. Weapons add a layer of consequence that the other cargo didn't carry — the knowledge that whatever you deliver will be used to hurt someone, and the uncertainty about who that someone will be.

choices.text: Take the weapons job — it's just cargo
Refuse weapons — you have limits

jsmg_023.title: The Weight of the Crate
text: The weapons crate is heavy in a way that transcends physics. You carry it through the scrap yards, past the rusting hulks of decommissioned machinery, and the weight sits in your arms and in your conscience simultaneously.

Pulse weapons. Military grade. Designed to disrupt neural systems — organic and synthetic — with a directed electromagnetic burst. Non-lethal at low settings, fatal at high. The kind of weapon that corporate security uses for crowd control and gangs use for everything else.

You deliver them. The warehouse in the old quarter smells of oil and metal. The buyer inspects the weapons with professional competence, counts them, tests one against a wall (the plaster vaporizes), and pays you without comment.

Three days later, a gang fight in Block 11 involves pulse weapons for the first time. Two bystanders are killed by stray electromagnetic bursts that stop their hearts. You read about it on a public data terminal and feel nothing, which is worse than feeling something, because feeling nothing means the line has moved and you didn't notice.

choices.text: The deaths aren't your responsibility. You moved cargo, nothing more
No more weapons. Find your line and hold it

jsmg_024.title: The Refusal Costs
text: You refuse the weapons job. The dealer takes it elsewhere. Nix doesn't comment on your decision directly, but her silence carries its own message: principles are a luxury, and in the smuggling business, luxury costs.

The cost is immediate. The weapons dealer was connected — his network of clients is extensive, and your refusal signals to them that you're selective, which in their world means unreliable. Two other potential clients withdraw pending jobs. Your income drops by a third.

But you sleep better. The particular insomnia of the smuggler — the 3 AM inventory of moral compromises — loosens its grip slightly. You moved medicine. You moved electronics. You moved a person to safety. You did not move the tools of murder. The line holds.
"You know what the difference between a smuggler and a criminal is?" Nix asks.
"What?"
"The smuggler thinks there's a difference."

choices.text: Accept the lower income and maintain your standards
Find ways to increase legitimate income to compensate

jsmg_025.title: The Dangerous Client
text: The client calls himself Vex. He operates from a private room in a club on the Neon Strip, surrounded by people who are simultaneously his entourage and his security detail. He wears expensive clothes badly, the way people do when they've acquired wealth faster than taste.

"I need someone to move forty kilos of Drift from the lower labs to a distribution point in the market district," Vex says. Drift — the neural stimulant that's been flooding the Lower District for the past year. Highly addictive, massively profitable, and responsible for a death toll that the district has stopped counting.
The pay is extraordinary. More than your last five jobs combined. But the cargo is poison, and you know it, and Vex knows you know it, and the question sits between you like a loaded weapon.
"Everybody needs a courier," Vex says. "Even the ones who sell bad things. Especially the ones who sell bad things."

choices.text: Refuse. Drift kills people and you won't be part of it
Take the job. The money is too good to pass up
Accept, but plan to sabotage the shipment

jsmg_026.title: Walking Away from Fortune
text: You refuse. Vex's expression doesn't change — he's heard no before, from people who are now employed elsewhere or not employed at all. "Your choice," he says. "But the offer stands. People change their minds when the rent comes due."

You walk out of the club and into the neon-stained night, and the credits you just refused feel like a phantom limb — gone but still aching. The Lower District's streets are full of Drift users, hollow-eyed and shaking, and you tell yourself that refusing the job makes a difference, but you know it doesn't. Vex will find another courier. The Drift will flow. The only thing your refusal changed is your bank balance.
But there are things you can live with and things you can't, and moving Drift falls on the wrong side of a line that keeps moving but hasn't moved that far. Not yet.

choices.text: Find legitimate courier work to make up the lost income
Build your own network from scratch. Independence beats loyalty

jsmg_027.title: The Drift Run
text: Forty kilos of Drift, packaged in sealed containers that look identical to industrial cleaning supplies. The weight is negligible. The moral weight is enormous. You carry it through the tunnels with the mechanical efficiency of a person who has decided not to think about what they're doing until after they've done it.

The delivery point is a basement in the market district. The recipients are efficient and professional — they unload, count, test, and pay without wasted motion. These are not street-level dealers. This is the middle tier of a distribution chain that stretches from chemical labs in the tunnels to users dying in containers throughout the district.

The credstick they hand you is heavy with credits. You pocket it and walk away and do not look back and do not think about the thousands of doses that your forty kilos will produce, or the thousands of people who will use them, or the hundreds who will become addicted, or the dozens who will die.
Not thinking about it is the job. You're getting very good at not thinking.

choices.text: Never again. One run was enough
The money is too good. Keep working for Vex

jsmg_028.title: The Saboteur
text: You accept the job and then betray it. In the tunnels, you open the containers and replace the Drift with an inert powder that looks identical — a trick Nix taught you months ago, for situations exactly like this. The real Drift you dump into the sewer water, where it dissolves into nothing.

The delivery goes smoothly. The recipients test a sample — you kept one real container for this purpose — and the test passes. They pay you. You walk away with a credstick full of drug money and the knowledge that forty kilos of Drift will never reach anyone's bloodstream.

The deception will be discovered. When the distribution chain processes the bulk of the shipment and finds inert powder instead of product, questions will be asked. Questions will lead to you. And Vex is not the kind of person who asks questions politely.
You have perhaps a week before the reckoning arrives.

choices.text: Prepare for Vex's retaliation
Strike first. Don't wait for Vex to come to you

jsmg_029.title: The Courier's Crossroads
text: Six months as a courier have taught you the geography of moral compromise. You know the routes — not just the physical ones, but the ethical ones. The paths between what you'll carry and what you won't. The distance between the person you were when you picked up your first package and the person you are now.

Nix sees the change. "You're at a crossroads," she says over drinks. "Couriers either stay couriers forever — comfortable, mid-level, safely anonymous — or they become something bigger. Kingpins. The people who run the routes instead of running them."
"What's the difference?"
"Couriers carry other people's risk. Kingpins carry their own. Couriers follow rules. Kingpins make them. Couriers can walk away. Kingpins can't."
She finishes her drink. "I'm retiring," she says. "My network needs a new operator. I'm offering it to you."

choices.text: Accept. Take over Nix's network
Negotiate terms. Taking over isn't automatic — set your conditions

jsmg_030.title: Rank 3: The Kingpin
text: You take the network. Nix's operation becomes yours — the contacts, the routes, the safe houses, the runners and couriers and warehouse operators who form the nervous system of the Lower District's smuggling infrastructure. You are no longer moving cargo. You are managing the movement of cargo. The distinction is everything.

The first week is chaos. Decisions come at you like bullets: route conflicts, client disputes, supply chain disruptions, personnel problems. A runner gets arrested and you have to determine if they'll talk and what to do about it. A client pays late and you have to decide whether to enforce payment or extend grace. A new patrol schedule renders three of your routes unusable overnight.

You handle it. Not gracefully — grace comes with experience — but effectively. The cargo flows. The credits flow. The network survives your first week in charge, and that is the only metric that matters.
Rank 3. Kingpin. The crown is heavy and it's made of compromise.

choices.text: Establish your authority and reshape the operation
Keep a low profile during the transition. Power grabs attract attention

jsmg_031.title: Building Your Empire
text: You restructure the network. New routes mapped by Ghost, who knows the tunnels better than the rats. New safe houses established in locations that corporate security hasn't catalogued. A communication system built on encrypted dead drops and analog codes that can't be digitally intercepted.

The operation professionalizes under your management. Runners are trained, not just hired. Routes are scouted and timed. Cargo is inspected and categorized. You bring logistics discipline to an industry that has traditionally operated on instinct and luck, and the results are immediate: fewer losses, faster deliveries, happier clients.

The Broker — the faceless voice that matches cargo with clients — expresses something almost like approval. "Your predecessor was good," the voice says through the encrypted channel. "You're efficient. Efficiency is rarer and more valuable."
"Efficient enough to take on more volume?"
"Efficient enough to attract attention. Be careful what you wish for."

choices.text: Expand carefully — controlled growth
Expand aggressively — take over new territory

jsmg_032.title: Controlled Growth
text: You grow slowly. New routes are tested with low-value cargo before being committed to serious shipments. New runners are vetted through a probation period. New clients are evaluated for risk and reliability before being added to the roster.

The approach is conservative and it works. Over three months, the network expands by thirty percent without a single interception. Your runners are well-trained, your routes are secure, and your clients receive their cargo on time, every time.

But conservative growth has a downside: it leaves space for competitors. And into that space steps Crane — the rival smuggler who hired Ferris as an informant. Crane has been building his own network, faster and less carefully, and his routes are beginning to overlap with yours.
The overlap means conflict. Two networks cannot operate the same corridors without colliding, and collision in the smuggling world doesn't involve lawyers.

choices.text: Negotiate with Crane — divide the territory
Confront Crane — this district isn't big enough for both of you

jsmg_033.title: Aggressive Expansion
text: You expand hard and fast. New routes opened weekly. New runners recruited from every block. New clients pursued with the aggressive salesmanship of a person who understands that market share is survival.

The speed produces results: doubled volume, tripled revenue, a network that covers every major corridor in the Lower District. But speed also produces mistakes. A runner gets caught because the route wasn't properly scouted. A shipment is delayed because the new warehouse wasn't adequately secured. A client receives damaged goods because the expanded logistics chain lacks quality control.

And the speed attracts exactly the attention the Broker warned about. Crane — the rival smuggler — watches your expansion with the calculating eye of a predator recognizing a threat. He's been building his own network, and your aggressive growth is pushing into territory he considers his.
War is coming. The only question is when.

choices.text: Prepare for the confrontation with Crane
Negotiate with Crane instead. War is expensive

jsmg_034.title: The Negotiation
text: You meet Crane in neutral territory — the Sector 7 bar, where the bartender ensures that nobody kills anyone during business hours. Crane is smaller than you expected, a wiry man with quick eyes and quicker hands, dressed in the practical clothing of someone who still runs his own routes.
"I know what you're going to propose," Crane says. "Territory division. You take the north corridors, I take the south. We stay out of each other's way and everyone makes money."
"That's the sensible option."

"Sensible. But not what I want." He leans forward. "I want a partnership. Merge the networks. My routes, your logistics. My speed, your discipline. Together we control every smuggling corridor in the Lower District."
A merger would create a monopoly — total control of the district's underground economy. The power would be immense. The risk would be concentrated in two people who don't fully trust each other.

choices.text: Accept the partnership — strength in unity
Insist on the territory division — independence matters more than power

jsmg_035.title: The Smuggler's War
text: Crane strikes first. Three of your runners are ambushed in a single night — different routes, coordinated timing. The cargo is taken, the runners are beaten (not killed, which is a message in itself: Crane is demonstrating capability, not savagery), and your network's reputation takes a hit.

You retaliate. Ghost identifies Crane's primary warehouse and you send a team to intercept an incoming shipment. The interception succeeds — you capture a week's worth of Crane's cargo and redistribute it through your own network. Crane's clients get their deliveries, just from a different supplier.

The war escalates in the particular way that smuggling wars do: not with bullets but with disruptions, interceptions, information warfare. Each side tries to undermine the other's operations while maintaining their own. It's chess played in sewer tunnels with stolen goods as pieces.

After three weeks, neither side is winning. Both networks are damaged. Both are bleeding revenue. The only beneficiary is the corporate patrol, which is picking off runners from both operations while the smugglers focus on each other.

choices.text: Propose a ceasefire — this war is killing both operations
Escalate — hit Crane's personal assets, end this definitively

jsmg_036.title: The Accord
text: The accord — whether merger or ceasefire — is hammered out over three days of negotiation. Territory divided. Routes assigned. Protocols established for overlap zones. A dispute resolution mechanism that doesn't involve violence, enforced by the mutual understanding that violence hurts profits.

Crane is a difficult partner but a competent one. His network fills gaps in yours — particularly in the industrial sectors, where his contacts among the foundry workers provide access to cargo channels you couldn't reach alone. In exchange, your logistics discipline improves his delivery rates.

The combined operation is formidable. Controlling the majority of the Lower District's smuggling infrastructure gives you influence over the flow of goods that the official economy doesn't provide: medicine, technology, food, information. Everything the Lower District needs and the corporate system won't supply.
You are now, by any reasonable definition, a power in the district.

choices.text: Manage the combined operation and consolidate power
Keep Crane at arm's length. Accords can be traps

jsmg_037.title: The Knockout Blow
text: You escalate. Ghost discovers Crane's personal safe house — the place where he keeps his reserves, his records, his fallback identity documents. Everything a smuggler needs to disappear if things go wrong.

You take it. Not with violence — with precision. While Crane is managing his network's defense, you infiltrate his safe house and remove everything: credits, documents, backup identities, blackmail material. You don't destroy it. You hold it.

When Crane realizes what's happened, the war ends. Not because he can't fight, but because you now hold everything he needs to survive outside the smuggling world. His retirement plan. His escape route. His insurance.
"I want your network," you tell him over the encrypted channel. "Your routes, your contacts, your infrastructure. In exchange, I give everything back and you walk away clean."
The silence on the channel lasts thirty seconds. "Done," Crane says. And it is.

choices.text: Absorb Crane's operation and consolidate your control
Offer Crane's people fair terms. Loyalty earned beats loyalty forced

jsmg_038.title: The Weight of Power
text: You control the routes. All of them. Every major smuggling corridor in the Lower District flows through your network. Medicine, technology, food, information, and yes — drugs, weapons, stolen goods. The full spectrum of the underground economy, managed by you, flowing through you.

The power is intoxicating and terrifying. You set prices, you choose clients, you decide what flows and what doesn't. When a community needs medicine, your network delivers it. When a gang needs weapons, your network supplies them. The moral ledger of your operation is a tangle of good and evil so intertwined that separating them would destroy both.

Nix, retired to a quiet container in Block 8, visits you occasionally. She looks at what you've built with an expression that blends pride and concern. "This is bigger than anything I ever ran," she says. "Be careful. The bigger the operation, the bigger the target. And the bigger the fall."
She's right. Corporate intelligence has noticed the consolidation. A single organization controlling the Lower District's underground economy is not a nuisance — it's a threat.

choices.text: Prepare defenses against corporate attention
Go on the offensive — disrupt corporate surveillance operations

jsmg_039.title: The Corporate Response
text: Meridian Security deploys a specialized team to the Lower District: a counter-smuggling unit staffed with former intelligence operatives and equipped with scanning technology that can detect your cargo through walls. They begin systematic sweeps of your routes, intercepting shipments, arresting runners, and dismantling safe houses.

In the first week, you lose three routes, two safe houses, and six runners. The financial hemorrhage is significant but the reputational damage is worse — clients see intercepted shipments and pull their business. The perception of invulnerability that kept competitors at bay begins to crack.

You adapt. Ghost reroutes the primary corridors through deeper tunnels that the scanners can't penetrate. You implement a cell structure so that captured runners can't compromise the wider network. You begin using decoy shipments to draw the corporate team away from real cargo.

The cat-and-mouse game accelerates. Every adaptation is met with a counter-adaptation. The corporate team is professional, well-funded, and motivated. But they don't know the district the way you do. They don't know the tunnels. They don't have Ghost.

choices.text: Outmaneuver them through superior local knowledge
Find the team's weakness and exploit it

jsmg_040.title: The Counter-Strike
text: Through the Data Haven, you learn that the counter-smuggling unit's leader is a woman named Captain Reis. Former military, extensive intelligence background, utterly committed to her mission. She has a weakness, though: her team is corporate, which means they're funded by a budget that can be cut.

You launch a dual strategy. Operationally, you continue the decoy-and-diversion tactics. Politically, you use your network's contacts in the corporate structure — sympathizers, compromised employees, people who owe favors — to question the unit's budget allocation. Is the cost of the counter-smuggling operation justified by its results? Are the intercepted shipments valuable enough to warrant the expense?

The corporate machine does what corporate machines do: it demands metrics. And the metrics — thanks to your decoy strategy — show the unit intercepting mostly worthless cargo while the real shipments flow unchecked. Reis is recalled for a budget review. Her team's operations pause.
You've bought time. Not victory — Reis will be back — but time enough to reinforce your network and prepare for the next assault.

choices.text: Use the breathing room to strengthen the network
Expand now while competitors are weakened

jsmg_041.title: Ghost's Discovery
text: Ghost finds something in the deep tunnels — a forgotten transit junction that connects the Lower District to the skyport through a route that bypasses every checkpoint, scanner, and patrol station. A direct line from the bottom of the city to its exit point, unsurveilled and unmonitored.

The route is dangerous. The tunnels are structurally unsound, flooded in sections, and home to wildlife that has adapted to the darkness in unsettling ways. But it's clean. Absolutely clean. No cameras, no patrols, no scanners.

"This changes everything," Ghost says, and for once the quiet man's voice carries emotion. "With this route, we can move cargo directly to the skyport. International shipping. Export. We go from district-level smuggling to global."

choices.text: Develop the skyport route — go global
Keep the route secret. A hidden exit is more valuable unused

jsmg_042.title: Going Global
text: You develop the route. It takes weeks of engineering — reinforcing tunnel sections, pumping out flooded passages, installing emergency lighting. Ghost supervises the work with the obsessive attention to detail of a man who has been mapping tunnels for twenty years and has finally found the one that matters.

The first shipment through the skyport route is a test: low-value cargo, minimal risk, maximum learning. The run takes four hours through unstable tunnels, past colonies of bioluminescent insects that have evolved in the perpetual darkness, through a section that groans with structural stress each time someone walks through it.

But it works. The cargo reaches the skyport. The skyport connects to international freight channels. And suddenly, your operation is no longer a Lower District smuggling network — it's an export business with global reach.

The Broker, for the first time, sounds genuinely interested. "You've just become the most valuable smuggling operation in the city. Congratulations. Everyone is going to want what you have. And some of them will try to take it."

choices.text: Secure the route and prepare for the attention it'll attract
Stay local. Global operations attract global enemies

jsmg_043.title: The Hidden Exit
text: You keep the route secret. Ghost is the only other person who knows it exists. An unused exit is the most valuable card in a smuggler's hand — the escape route that nobody can block because nobody knows it's there.

Instead of developing it commercially, you use it sparingly: emergency evacuations, critical shipments that absolutely cannot be intercepted, and — most importantly — the movement of people. Refugees, whistleblowers, fugitives. People who need to leave the city and can't use official channels.
Adaeze was the first. There will be others. And for each one, the skyport route provides something that no amount of money can buy: a way out. A last resort. A tunnel to the sky.
The route remains your secret weapon, unused except when lives depend on it, its value measured not in credits but in the people who walk through it to safety.

choices.text: Continue building the network with this ace up your sleeve
Share the exit with key allies. Trust builds stronger networks

jsmg_044.title: The Betrayal
text: The betrayal comes from inside, as betrayals always do. One of your senior couriers — a woman named Jade, who has been with the network since Nix's era — is turned by Captain Reis. The corporate intelligence officer offers Jade immunity and relocation in exchange for the network's full operational map: routes, safe houses, schedules, codes.

You discover the betrayal through the Broker, whose information network extends even into Meridian Security's internal communications. "Your courier Jade met with Reis three times this week," the Broker informs you. "She's providing operational details. You have perhaps forty-eight hours before Reis has enough to shut you down."
Forty-eight hours. The clock starts now.

choices.text: Feed Jade false information to misdirect Reis
Shut everything down. Go completely dark

jsmg_045.title: The Disinformation Campaign
text: You don't confront Jade. Instead, you use her. You create a phantom network — false routes, false safe houses, false schedules — and feed it to her through the normal channels. She passes it to Reis. Reis deploys her team based on the false intelligence.

The result is beautiful in its precision. Reis's counter-smuggling unit raids six locations and finds nothing. They patrol three routes and intercept zero shipments. They stake out two safe houses that are actually empty storage containers. The unit's credibility collapses. Reis's superiors question her intelligence sources.

Meanwhile, your real operations continue through routes that Jade doesn't know about — routes you compartmentalized precisely for this scenario. The network functions at reduced capacity but remains undetected.

Jade is arrested a week later, not by you but by Reis, who has realized that her informant was providing garbage intelligence. The irony is complete: the betrayer is punished by the entity she betrayed you to.

choices.text: Rebuild the network with better security
Purge everyone who might be compromised. Security demands ruthlessness

jsmg_046.title: Going Dark
text: You shut everything down in twenty-four hours. Every route closed. Every safe house evacuated. Every runner given a shutdown code and told to disappear until further notice. The network that took months to build vanishes overnight, as if it never existed.

Reis's team arrives to find nothing. Empty tunnels, abandoned warehouses, cold trails. The operational map that Jade provided is accurate but useless — every location it identifies is empty. The network has evaporated.

For three weeks, the Lower District's underground economy grinds to a halt. No smuggled medicine. No diverted food supplies. No information flowing through the unofficial channels that keep the district connected. The community feels the absence like a severed limb.

Then, slowly, carefully, you rebuild. Not the same network — that one is compromised. A new one, designed from the ground up with compartmentalization that would survive even a senior courier's betrayal. Each cell knows only its own routes. No single person can compromise the whole.
It's smaller. It's slower. But it's unbreakable.

choices.text: Begin the rebuilding process
Build the new network on completely different principles

jsmg_047.title: The Question of Legacy
text: The network is rebuilt, stronger than before, and the question that has been growing since your first run crystallizes into something you can no longer ignore: what is all of this for?

You have built the most efficient smuggling operation in the district's history. You have moved medicine, food, technology, weapons, drugs, and people through tunnels and corridors and checkpoints. You have made money. You have broken laws. You have done good and done harm in measures you've stopped trying to balance.
Nix, in her quiet retirement, asks you the question directly: "What do you want to be when this is over? A legend? A cautionary tale? A ghost who disappears when the work is done?"
The answer defines your ending.
Rank 3 is complete. The final rank approaches — and with it, the question of what your network becomes.

choices.text: Enter the final phase. Decide the network's future
Walk away now. Leave the legacy question to someone else

jsmg_048.title: Rank 4: The Legend
text: They call you the Conduit. Not to your face — nobody in the smuggling world uses real names or real titles to someone's face — but in whispers, in code names, in the particular reverence that the underground reserves for those who have survived long enough to become mythological.

You control every major smuggling corridor in the Lower District. Your network supplies the community with everything the official economy denies them. You are, in practical terms, the shadow government of the district's commerce — the invisible hand that ensures the forgotten people of the lower city have access to the goods and services that the corporate system withholds.
But the Conduit stands at a crossroads. The network you've built can serve many futures, and the future you choose determines not just your legacy but the district's.
Rank 4. Legend. The final chapter.

choices.text: Consider the four paths before you
Take time to think. This decision shapes everything that follows

jsmg_049.title: Four Futures
text: You sit in the Sector 7 bar where it all began and you see the four paths clearly.

You can become the Trade Master — legitimize the network, transform it from a criminal enterprise into an alternative economy that operates openly alongside the corporate system. Legal, transparent, and powerful in a different way.

You can become the Phantom — disappear, destroy all evidence of your involvement, and let the network operate independently. Ghost in the machine. The legend who was never caught because nobody could prove they existed.

You can burn it down — destroy the smuggling infrastructure entirely, forcing the district to find new solutions and eliminating the criminal element that your network has become. A controlled demolition of your own empire.
Or you can keep going — expand beyond the district, beyond the city, build a smuggling empire that reaches the entire sector. Go from kingpin to emperor.

choices.text: The Trade Master — legitimize everything
The Phantom — disappear into legend
Burn it down — destroy the network
The Emperor — expand and build an empire

jsmg_050.title: The Trade Master Path — Part 1
text: Legitimization begins with transparency. You approach the district's community council with a proposal: recognize the underground trade network as a legitimate commercial operation. Replace smuggling routes with trade corridors. Replace black market transactions with registered commerce. Replace the secrecy with accountability.
The council is skeptical. "You want to take a criminal enterprise and make it legal by declaration?"

"I want to take the economy that actually works and give it official standing," you correct. "The corporate supply chain doesn't serve this district. Ours does. The only thing making it illegal is that nobody with authority has acknowledged it exists."

choices.text: Continue making the case for legitimization
Demonstrate value through action, not arguments

jsmg_051.title: The Trade Master Path — Part 2
text: The legitimization process takes months. You register trade routes as commercial corridors. You establish quality controls for goods that previously flowed unregulated. You create a transparent pricing structure that replaces the black market's chaotic haggling with something approaching fair commerce.

Not everything can be legitimized. The drug trade, the weapons trade, the movement of people — these remain underground, operated by fragments of the old network that refuse to come into the light. You don't control them anymore, and the loss of control means the loss of the moral oversight — however imperfect — that your management provided.

But the legal trade flourishes. Medicine, technology, food, information — all flowing through official channels that the corporate system can't shut down without admitting that the Lower District has built an alternative economy that works better than theirs.

choices.text: Push for full recognition of the trade network
Accept partial recognition. Perfect is the enemy of good

jsmg_052.title: The Trade Master's Legacy
text: The Lower District Free Trade Zone opens on a day when the rain pauses long enough for the ceremony to feel celebratory rather than defiant. You stand at the entrance of the market district's new commercial center — built from the same shipping containers and salvaged materials that housed your smuggling operation, now openly labeled and legally recognized.

The transition from smuggler to merchant is not as dramatic as you expected. You still move goods from place to place. You still negotiate with suppliers and clients. You still solve logistics problems with creativity and persistence. The only difference is that now you do it in the light, and the thing you carry is not contraband but commerce.

Nix attends the opening. She stands in the crowd and watches the district's first legal trade market with an expression that is either pride or amusement or both. "You made it honest," she says. "I never thought that was possible."
"It was always honest," you reply. "The work was the same. The laws just caught up."

The Conduit is gone. In their place stands the Trade Master — a title that means the same thing without the risk of imprisonment. The evolution is complete, and the district's economy, for the first time, belongs to the people who built it.

jsmg_053.title: Night Operations
text: The night runs continue even as the legitimate trade grows. Some cargo cannot be moved in daylight, through official channels, under registered manifests. Medical supplies diverted from corporate warehouses. Technology components embargoed by trade regulations. Food stockpiles from sources that prefer anonymity.

You maintain a skeleton crew for the night operations — Ghost, two trusted runners, and a warehouse operator who handles the dark-side logistics. The dual nature of your operation is a constant tension: by day, the legitimate trade master; by night, the smuggler who never fully left the tunnels.

The duality suits you. The light side provides stability, income, community standing. The dark side provides the tools to do what the light side cannot — reach beyond the law to serve needs that the law doesn't recognize.

choices.text: Keep the balance between light and dark operations
End the night operations entirely. Full legitimacy or nothing

jsmg_054.title: A Runner's Last Run
text: A young runner on your night crew gets caught by corporate security. Not arrested — killed. A patrol officer who panicked, a weapon discharged, a body in a tunnel. The runner was seventeen.

You sit with the runner's mother in the clinic and you look at your hands and you think: these are the hands that built the network that put a seventeen-year-old in a tunnel at 3 AM. These are the hands that created the system that killed him.

The guilt is familiar. You've carried it since your first run, when you chose to enter the tunnels with a package and a prayer. Every runner you've hired, every route you've opened, every operation you've built has been a calculated risk with other people's lives. The calculation has always worked — until now.
The mother asks you: "Was it worth it? Everything he carried, everyone he helped — was it worth his life?"
You don't have an answer. You have only the weight of the question, and it will stay with you for as long as you carry cargo through the dark.

choices.text: Honor the runner's sacrifice and continue the work
Retire the dangerous routes. No cargo is worth a life

jsmg_055.title: Supply Line Ethics
text: A new client offers an ongoing contract: move biological samples from a research facility to an independent lab. The samples are human tissue — legally obtained, according to the client, from consenting donors. The pay is excellent. The cargo is disturbing in a way that makes you question the nature of consent in a city where poverty makes everything negotiable.

You investigate. The samples are from Stack donors — people who sold biological material in exchange for credits, in transactions that are technically legal but ethically murky. The research being conducted is legitimate: genetic therapy for a degenerative disease that affects the Lower District's population disproportionately.
The good of the research against the exploitation of the donors. Another moral equation with no clean solution.

choices.text: Take the contract — the research could save lives
Refuse — you don't move people or pieces of people

jsmg_056.title: The Network's Conscience
text: You implement a code. Not the unwritten rules that every smuggling operation develops organically, but a formal ethical framework that governs what your network will and won't carry. No Drift. No weapons intended for use against civilians. No stolen cortical stacks. No cargo that is a person who hasn't consented to being moved.

The code costs you clients. The weapons dealers go elsewhere. The drug runners find other networks. The revenue drops by a quarter, and some of your operators grumble about principles being expensive.

But the code also attracts clients. The kind of clients who want reliability and integrity in their smuggling — a paradox that only makes sense in the Lower District, where the official economy is so corrupt that the criminal economy becomes the ethical alternative.
Ghost approves. "A network without a code is just organized crime," he says. "A network with a code is... still organized crime, but the organized kind that helps people."

choices.text: Enforce the code strictly as the network grows
Allow exceptions for desperate cases. Rigid codes break under pressure

jsmg_057.title: The Broker Revealed
text: The Broker requests a meeting. In person. For the first time in the years you've worked together, the faceless voice wants a face-to-face conversation.

You meet in the Data Haven, surrounded by the hum of servers and the blue glow of data streams. The Broker is not what you expected. She is elderly, white-haired, frail-looking in a way that belies the power she wields. Her name, she tells you, is Madame Chen, and she has been coordinating smuggling operations across the city for thirty years.

"I'm retiring," she says. "Not by choice. My health is failing, and the work requires a clarity I can no longer consistently provide. I need someone to take over the coordination role. Someone who understands logistics, ethics, and the particular balance of power that keeps the underground economy functioning."
She looks at you. "I'm asking you."

choices.text: Accept — become the new Broker
Decline — you're a hands-on operator, not a coordinator

jsmg_058.title: The New Broker
text: You accept. The transition from field operator to coordinator is jarring — you go from running physical routes to managing information streams, from carrying cargo to matching it with carriers, from the visceral reality of tunnels and checkpoints to the abstract reality of data flows and network optimization.

But the scope is extraordinary. As Broker, you don't just manage your own network — you coordinate the entire underground economy of the city. Every smuggling operation, every independent runner, every black market dealer flows through your matching system. You see the full picture for the first time, and the picture is vast.

The Lower District's underground economy is not an aberration. It is the economy. The official corporate system handles perhaps thirty percent of the actual goods flowing through the district. The remaining seventy percent flows through you.
The weight of that knowledge is staggering. You are not a smuggler anymore. You are the architecture that makes survival possible for a hundred thousand people.

choices.text: Embrace the role and shape the underground economy
Keep your hands dirty. Brokers who forget the ground lose their edge

jsmg_059.title: The Hands-On Operator
text: You decline. Madame Chen nods without surprise — she's been reading people for thirty years and she read your answer before you gave it. She finds another successor, someone with the temperament for coordination, and you return to the tunnels.

The tunnels are where you belong. Not the data streams, not the coordination networks, not the abstract management of information flows. The physical space, the dark corridors, the weight of cargo in your hands. You are a smuggler, and the word means something specific: a person who moves things through spaces where they're not supposed to go.

Ghost joins you for a run, the way he used to before the network grew so large that personal runs became a luxury. The two of you carry medical supplies through the deep tunnels, and for a few hours, everything is simple again. Cargo. Route. Delivery. The fundamental equation of the work you chose.

choices.text: Continue running. This is who you are
Accept that running has an end date. Plan for what comes after

jsmg_060.title: The Phantom Path — Part 1
text: You decide to disappear. Not flee — disappear. Remove every trace of your involvement in the network, every connection to your identity, every piece of evidence that the person called the Conduit ever existed. Become the smuggler who was never caught because nobody could prove there was a smuggler to catch.

The process is meticulous. You transfer operational control to Ghost, who is the only person you trust completely. You burn records, wipe databases, destroy the physical artifacts of your tenure. You create a layered succession plan so that the network functions without central leadership, each cell operating independently.

The hardest part is saying goodbye to Nix. She understands — she's the one who taught you that the best smuggling career is the one nobody knows about — but understanding and accepting are different things.

choices.text: Complete the disappearance
Leave a trail for one trusted person. Phantoms need emergency contacts

jsmg_061.title: The Phantom Path — Part 2
text: You walk out of the Lower District through the skyport route — Ghost's discovery, the tunnel to the sky. You carry nothing. No cargo, no credstick, no evidence of the life you built. Just yourself and the knowledge of how to move through spaces unseen.

Behind you, the network continues. Ghost runs it with quiet competence. The runners run their routes. The cargo flows. The community receives the goods it needs. The system you built functions without you, which is the highest compliment an architect can receive: a building that stands after the builder walks away.

Captain Reis searches for the Conduit for six more months before her unit is reassigned. She never finds them. The Conduit becomes a legend — the smuggler who controlled everything and then vanished, leaving behind a network that operates like a ghost in the machine, present everywhere and nowhere.

choices.text: Walk into a new life
Look back. Once. Just to remember who you were

jsmg_062.title: The Phantom's Legacy
text: Years later, stories are told in the Lower District about the Conduit. They grow with each telling, the way stories do, accumulating details and drama until the real person is buried under layers of legend. The smuggler who moved a human being through corporate security like a ghost. The operator who outsmarted Meridian Intelligence. The kingpin who built an empire and then burned it behind them as they walked into the sunrise.
None of the stories are quite right. All of them are true enough.

You stand at a skyport somewhere far from the city, watching ships lift off for destinations you could never have imagined when you picked up your first package in a maintenance tunnel beneath Platform 7. You are nobody now. No reputation, no network, no history. Just a person with steady hands and a working knowledge of how to move through the dark.
The bartender at the skyport lounge slides you a drink and asks where you're headed.
"Everywhere," you say. "Nowhere. Whatever comes next."
The Conduit is gone. The legend remains. And somewhere in the Lower District, cargo still flows through tunnels that bear no name and no signature, moved by hands that learned from yours.

jsmg_063.title: Ghost Routes
text: Even after your disappearance, the routes you mapped remain. Ghost maintains them with the reverence of a curator preserving art. Each tunnel, each passage, each junction point is a piece of your legacy — the physical infrastructure of survival that you carved through the city's underbelly.

New runners learn the routes without knowing who created them. They pass through tunnels you discovered, use safe houses you established, follow protocols you designed. Your name is never mentioned because your name no longer exists, but your work is everywhere.

choices.text: The routes endure. That is enough
Improve the routes before abandoning them. Leave them better than you found them

jsmg_064.title: Emergency Recall
text: Ghost contacts you through the emergency channel — a dead drop system so deeply buried that using it means the situation is critical. The message is brief: 'Network compromised. Reis returned with reinforcements. Need tactical guidance.'

You respond from a distance, providing strategic advice without revealing your location. The advice works — Ghost implements your suggestions and the network survives the assault. But the encounter reminds you that disappearing doesn't mean disconnecting. The network you built still carries your fingerprints, and when it's threatened, the phantom is called back to haunt.

choices.text: Help from the shadows, then fade again
Refuse the recall. You're out. Stay out

jsmg_065.title: The Runner's Apprentice
text: Before you disappeared, you trained one last runner — a teenager named Kite who reminded you of yourself. Quick, resourceful, hungry in the way that poverty makes people hungry. You taught Kite everything: the routes, the techniques, the rules, and the one lesson that matters most.
"Don't ask what's in the package," you told her. "But always be ready for the moment when you do."

choices.text: Kite carries your legacy forward
Warn Kite about the mistakes you made. Pass on failures, not just techniques

jsmg_066.title: The New Identity
text: Your new identity is clean — no criminal record, no smuggling connections, no history in the Lower District. You are a person born from documents, existing on paper and in databases, with a past that was manufactured by the same forgers who create travel documents for refugees.

The new life is quiet. Legitimate work, legitimate income, legitimate existence. You live in a mid-level apartment that has windows and sunlight, two things that the Lower District rarely provides. You eat food that comes from registered suppliers through legal channels.
It should feel like victory. Instead, it feels like exile.

choices.text: Accept the quiet life. You've earned it
Keep one smuggling skill sharp. Just in case

jsmg_067.title: Watching from the Outside
text: From your new life, you watch the Lower District from the outside. The network you built continues to function — you can tell by the flow of goods in the market, by the availability of medicine in the clinics, by the subtle indicators that only a former smuggler would recognize.

Ghost is doing well. The network is smaller than during your tenure but more resilient. The ethical code you established is being maintained. The community is surviving, the way it always has, through the unofficial channels that keep the forgotten people of the lower city alive.
You are proud. And you are lonely. And the two feelings coexist in a way that is simultaneously complete and insufficient.

choices.text: Make peace with the distance
Close the distance. Watch from nearby, not from far away

jsmg_068.title: One Last Run
text: You can't stay away. One night, unable to sleep, you return to the tunnels. Not to run cargo — just to walk. The familiar darkness, the smell of rust and water, the sound of your footsteps on metal grating. The tunnel is a place where you were most fully yourself, and leaving it left a hole that no amount of sunlight and legitimate work can fill.

You walk the old routes, remembering. Here is where you bluffed the patrol. There is where the hijackers struck. That junction is where Ghost found the skyport route. Every corridor holds a story, and the stories are yours, and they matter more than the quiet life you've built above.

A young runner passes you in the dark, carrying a package, moving with the careful speed of someone early in their career. They don't recognize you. Nobody does. You are the ghost in the tunnels that built the ghost in the machine.

choices.text: Return to the surface. The tunnels remember you. That's enough
Run again. One last time, for real. The tunnels call

jsmg_069.title: The Legend Lives
text: Children in the Lower District play a game called 'Conduit.' They run through the corridors carrying small packages, dodging imaginary patrols, delivering invisible cargo to secret locations. They argue about the Conduit's identity — some say it was a man, some say a woman, some say a synth, some say nobody at all.

The legend has taken on its own life, and the person behind it has been absorbed into the story. You are no longer needed. The idea of you — the smuggler who served the community and then vanished — serves a purpose that transcends your physical presence. It gives the children a hero. It gives the runners a standard. It gives the district a reminder that one person can build something that matters.
You hear about the game from a distance and you smile. The first genuine smile in months. The person is gone. The legend endures. And the cargo still flows.

choices.text: The story is the best legacy
Correct the legends. Truth matters, even about smugglers

jsmg_070.title: The Demolition Path — Part 1
text: You decide to destroy it. All of it. The routes, the safe houses, the communication networks, the supply chains. The entire smuggling infrastructure you've spent years building. Not because it's failed but because of what it's become — a criminal enterprise that, despite your ethical code, facilitates as much harm as good.

The weapons still flow. The drugs still flow. The exploitation still flows. Your code limits it but doesn't eliminate it, and the network you built provides the infrastructure for every kind of trafficking that operates in the district. You are not just a smuggler — you are the enabling architecture of every smuggler.
"If I destroy the network," you tell Ghost, "the district loses its supply chain. Medicine, food, technology — all of it stops."

Ghost looks at you with the quiet assessment of a man who has lived in the tunnels for twenty years. "Then the district builds a new one. Without the criminal infrastructure. Something better, maybe."

choices.text: Begin the controlled demolition
Preserve some of the network's infrastructure for the community

jsmg_071.title: The Demolition Path — Part 2
text: The demolition is surgical. You don't destroy everything at once — that would cause a supply crisis that could kill people. Instead, you dismantle the network piece by piece, transferring legitimate supply chains to community organizations, shutting down criminal channels, and collapsing the routes that serve no constructive purpose.

The drug channels go first. Then the weapons channels. Then the stolen goods networks. Each closure leaves a gap that the market tries to fill, and each gap that remains unfilled is a criminal operation that no longer exists.

The legitimate channels — medicine, food, technology — you transfer to the district's community council, with documentation, contacts, and operating procedures. The transition is messy but functional. The council inherits a working supply chain without the criminal overhead.
By the time you're done, the underground economy has been split in two: the legitimate portion transferred to community control, and the criminal portion destroyed.

choices.text: See the demolition through to completion
Sabotage the demolition. Some things shouldn't be destroyed

jsmg_072.title: The Demolisher's Legacy
text: The network is gone. The tunnels are quiet. The safe houses are empty. The communication channels carry nothing but static. The most efficient smuggling operation in the Lower District's history has been erased by the person who built it.

The aftermath is complicated. New criminal networks emerge to fill the void, smaller and less organized, without your ethical code. The community-managed supply chain struggles with logistics that your network handled effortlessly. People suffer during the transition — delayed medicine, interrupted food supplies, disrupted information flows.
But slowly, the community adapts. It builds new systems, legitimate ones, without the criminal foundation that yours was built on. The process is painful and imperfect and necessary.
Nix calls you an idiot. "You destroyed a machine that worked," she says. "You burned it down because it wasn't pure enough. Nothing is ever pure enough."

She might be right. But the machine that replaces yours will be built by the community, owned by the community, and operated without the compromises that haunted every decision you made. That has to be worth the pain of the transition.
You walk away from the tunnels for the last time. The darkness behind you is just darkness now — no routes, no cargo, no legend. Just empty space, waiting to be filled with something better.

jsmg_073.title: Rebuilding Aftermath
text: The community's new supply system takes shape slowly. Without the efficiency of your network, deliveries are slower, costlier, and less reliable. But they are clean — no drug piggybacking, no weapons shipments hidden in medical crates, no moral compromises baked into the infrastructure.

You help from the sidelines, offering logistical advice without taking operational control. The temptation to rebuild is constant — you see inefficiencies that you could fix, routes that you could optimize, connections that you could establish. But rebuilding would recreate the very thing you destroyed.
Restraint, you discover, is harder than demolition.

choices.text: Stay on the sidelines. Let the community build its own way
Help build the replacement. You know what the community needs

jsmg_074.title: Ghosts of the Old Network
text: Former members of your network adapt in different ways. Ghost becomes a consultant for the community council's logistics team. The Broker — Madame Chen — retires fully, her coordination services no longer needed. The runners scatter: some find legitimate work, some join the smaller criminal networks that emerged, some leave the district entirely.

You feel responsible for all of them. The network was more than an operation — it was an employer, a community, a family of sorts. Destroying it displaced dozens of people who had built their lives around the work it provided.
